_provenance.note: This Stage 1 JSON was reconstructed from the currently-published cards so external Stage 2 (Gemini bias) and Stage 3 (Codex fact-check) reviews can verify what readers see. It is not the original analysis — the original pipeline was not run on this issue.

headline: DOE Enforcement Officers Cut by 18% Despite Rising Pollution
context: Parliamentary records show DOE's enforcement headcount fell from 892 in 2019 to 732 in 2025, an 18% reduction, while registered pollution sources increased 24% in the same period.

cards.text: DOE lost 160 enforcement officers in 6 years.
cards.sub: In the same period, registered factories increased by 12,400. Each officer now covers 47% more pollution sources.

cards.h: DOE's 2025 enforcement budget is RM42M, down 12% in real terms from 2019.
cards.s: Environmental crimes generate an estimated RM8.4B in economic damage annually.
cards.d: DOE's 2025 enforcement budget is RM42M, down 12% in real terms from 2019. Environmental crimes generate an estimated RM8.4B in economic damage annually.

cards.h: Prosecution rate for environmental offences fell from 8.2% to 4.1% since 2019.
cards.s: Fewer officers means fewer inspections, fewer samples, fewer prosecutable cases.
cards.d: Prosecution rate for environmental offences fell from 8.2% to 4.1% since 2019. Fewer officers means fewer inspections, fewer samples, fewer prosecutable cases.

cards.h: Singapore's NEA has 2,800 enforcement officers for a country 1/450th Malaysia's size.
cards.s: Per-area enforcement density in Malaysia is approximately 0.3% of Singapore's.
cards.d: Singapore's NEA has 2,800 enforcement officers for a country 1/450th Malaysia's size. Per-area enforcement density in Malaysia is approximately 0.3% of Singapore's.

cards.text: Cutting enforcement while pollution rises is not a budget decision. It is an environmental policy.
Like reducing the number of traffic police while accident rates climb. The budget line shrinks, but the cost transfers to hospitals and families.
cards.sub: Enforcement cuts during rising pollution shift the cost from the DOE budget to public health and environmental damage

cards.text: Environmental law is only as strong as the people who enforce it. Malaysia's pollution problem is not primarily legal. It is an enforcement capacity crisis.
